title: Lucy Kirkwood

infobox.value: [FRSL](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Literature "Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature") (born c. 1984) is a British [playwright](/wiki/Playwright "Playwright") and screenwriter. She is known for her plays *[Chimerica](/wiki/Chimerica_(play) "Chimerica (play)")* (2013) and  *[The Children](/wiki/The_Children_(play) "The Children (play)")* (2016).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British playwright and screenwriter

**Lucy Ann Kirkwood** [FRSL](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Literature "Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature") (born c. 1984) is a British [playwright](/wiki/Playwright "Playwright") and screenwriter. She is known for her plays *[Chimerica](/wiki/Chimerica_(play) "Chimerica (play)")* (2013) and  *[The Children](/wiki/The_Children_(play) "The Children (play)")* (2016).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucy_Kirkwood&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Kirkwood was born in [Leytonstone](/wiki/Leytonstone "Leytonstone") around 1984 and raised in [east London](/wiki/East_London "East London"). She has a degree in English literature from the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh"), where she performed as part of an improvisational comedy troupe, the [Improverts](/wiki/Improverts "Improverts") and wrote for the [Edinburgh University Theatre Company](/wiki/Edinburgh_University_Theatre_Company "Edinburgh University Theatre Company").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucy_Kirkwood&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Plays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucy_Kirkwood&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Plays")]

In 2005, Kirkwood wrote and starred in her first play, *Grady Hot Potato*, at the [Bedlam Theatre](/wiki/Bedlam_Theatre "Bedlam Theatre"). It was also selected for the [National Student Drama Festival](/wiki/National_Student_Drama_Festival "National Student Drama Festival").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The following year, in 2006, Kirkwood took two productions of her second play, *Geronimo* to the [Edinburgh Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe"), under the title *The Umbilical Project*. The two productions, *Cut* and *Uncut*, were an experiment in cutting the cord between writer and production. *Uncut* was directed by Kirkwood herself and *Cut* by a completely separate company under the direction of Matt Addicott. No contact was made between the two companies during the rehearsal period, prompting the tag line "Two casts, two crews, two directors, two venues, one new play... no communication".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Kirkwood's third play, *Guns or Butter*, about soldiers being overcome by the horror of war, was written for the Terror 2007 Festival at the [Union Theatre](/wiki/Union_Theatre_(London) "Union Theatre (London)"), London.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

*Tinderbox*, a dark comedy set in a fictional 21st-century England, premiered at the [Bush Theatre](/wiki/Bush_Theatre "Bush Theatre") in April 2008 starring [Jamie Foreman](/wiki/Jamie_Foreman "Jamie Foreman") and [Sheridan Smith](/wiki/Sheridan_Smith "Sheridan Smith").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) She also contributed to the Bush's *50 Ways to Leave Your Lover*, a play co-created in collaboration with Leah Chillery, [Ben Ellis](/wiki/Ben_Ellis_(playwright) "Ben Ellis (playwright)"), Stacey Gregg, [Morgan Lloyd Malcolm](/wiki/Morgan_Lloyd_Malcolm "Morgan Lloyd Malcolm") and [Ben Schiffer](/wiki/Ben_Schiffer "Ben Schiffer") which debuted in July 2008, and was revised and restaged in December of the same year.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Her short horror piece *Psychogeography* premiered at the Bush in 2008 and was revived at [Southwark Playhouse](/wiki/Southwark_Playhouse "Southwark Playhouse").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Her version of [Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen")'s *[Hedda Gabler](/wiki/Hedda_Gabler "Hedda Gabler")*, titled *Hedda*, premiered at London's [Gate Theatre](/wiki/Gate_Theatre_(London) "Gate Theatre (London)") in 2008 to favourable reviews.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Kirkwood's promenade play about sex trafficking, *It Felt Empty When the Heart Went At First but It Is Alright Now*,[[8]](#cite_note-8) was produced by Clean Break Theatre at the [Arcola Theatre](/wiki/Arcola_Theatre "Arcola Theatre") in October 2009.

Her short play *Bloody Wimmin* debuted at the Tricycle Theater (now the [Kiln Theatre](/wiki/Kiln_Theatre "Kiln Theatre")) as part of their *Women, Power and Politics* season in June 2010.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Additionally, a fresh and humorous version of *[Beauty and the Beast](/wiki/Beauty_and_the_Beast "Beauty and the Beast")* was devised by Kirkwood and director Katie Mitchell and written by Kirkwood. It premiered at the [National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") in London as Christmas show in December 2010.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In January 2011 her one-woman short play *Small Hours* debuted at [Hampstead Theatre](/wiki/Hampstead_Theatre "Hampstead Theatre").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In October 2012, Kirkwood's play *NSFW* premiered at the [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), starring [Janie Dee](/wiki/Janie_Dee "Janie Dee") and [Julian Barrett](/wiki/Julian_Barratt "Julian Barratt") and directed by [Simon Godwin](/wiki/Simon_Godwin "Simon Godwin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In May 2013, Kirkwood's play *[Chimerica](/wiki/Chimerica_(play) "Chimerica (play)")*, which examines the relationship between the US and China since the [Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square_protests_of_1989 "Tiananmen Square protests of 1989") through the eyes of a former activist, and features over forty scene changes and British-Chinese actors, opened at the [Almeida Theatre](/wiki/Almeida_Theatre "Almeida Theatre"). The play transferred to the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") a few months later, in August 2013. The play's title echoes the portmanteau word "[Chimerica](/wiki/Chimerica "Chimerica")", invented by economists to define the intertwined economies of the US and China.[[12]](#cite_note-12) At the [2014 Olivier Awards](/wiki/2014_Laurence_Olivier_Awards "2014 Laurence Olivier Awards"), *Chimerica* won for best new play, best director (Lyndsey Turner), best lighting (Tim Lutkin and Finn Ross), best sound (Carolyn Downing), and best set design (Es Devlin).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Kirkwood was [writer in residence](/wiki/Writer_in_residence "Writer in residence") at [Clean Break](/wiki/Clean_Break_(theatre_company) "Clean Break (theatre company)"), a theatre company focused on telling the stories of imprisoned women, in 2015.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Kirkwood's play *[The Children](/wiki/The_Children_(play) "The Children (play)")* opened at the [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre") in November 2016, directed by James Macdonald and starring [Ron Cook](/wiki/Ron_Cook "Ron Cook"), [Francesca Annis](/wiki/Francesca_Annis "Francesca Annis") and [Deborah Findlay](/wiki/Deborah_Findlay "Deborah Findlay"), receiving positive reviews from critics.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The production transferred to the [Samuel J. Friedman Theatre](/wiki/Samuel_J._Friedman_Theatre "Samuel J. Friedman Theatre") on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") in November 2017 (previews), officially on 12 December 2017, with its original cast.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The play was nominated for the 2018 [Outer Critics Circle Award](/wiki/Outer_Critics_Circle_Award "Outer Critics Circle Award") as Outstanding New Broadway Play.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The play was nominated for the 2018 [Tony Award for Best Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Play "Tony Award for Best Play") and the Tony Award, Best Performance by an Actress in a Featured Role in a Play (Deborah Findlay).[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[Rufus Norris](/wiki/Rufus_Norris "Rufus Norris") directed her stage play *Mosquitoes*, which illustrates family disputes between sisters by referencing [collisions](/wiki/Annihilation "Annihilation") in [experimental physics](/wiki/Experimental_physics "Experimental physics"), at London's [National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") in 2017.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Kirkwood received a commissioning grant from the [Manhattan Theatre Club](/wiki/Manhattan_Theatre_Club "Manhattan Theatre Club") to write the play.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Thomas Bockelmann directed *Mosquitoes* at the German [Staatstheater Kassel](/wiki/Staatstheater_Kassel "Staatstheater Kassel") on 30 August 2018 as a three-hour stage production.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Kirkwood's next play was titled *[The Welkin](/w/index.php?title=The_Welkin&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Welkin (page does not exist)")* and concerned the case of a woman convicted of murder who claims to be pregnant, preventing a death sentence from being carried out. The play revolves around a group of 12 matrons assembled to determine the truth of her claim. *The Welkin* was performed at London's National Theatre in the first half of 2020.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 2021, Kirkwood wrote the play *Maryland*, which was staged shortly after at the Royal Court Theatre, and later adapted into a BBC dramatic television film.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In June 2022, Kirkwood's play *Rapture*, opened at the [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), credited in the marketing under the pseudonym Dave Davidson, and the false title *That Is Not Who I Am*. The misleading advertisement ties into the themes and world of the story. The play purports to be a [verbatim docudrama](/wiki/Verbatim_theatre "Verbatim theatre") concerning paranoia and distrust of the UK Government following the 2020 pandemic and ensuing political scandals. It details the relationship, surveillance, and murder of two climate activist content creators, leaving lingering questions about the State's involvement. In the universe of this play, the UK Government has placed an embargo on the Royal Court (or theatre in question) over the contents presented, and the legality of its production are in question. Thus the opening moments of the play include an explanation that the theatre has decided to stage the play covertly. It features both an actor playing Kirkwood and a supposed cameo from the real Kirkwood herself. Kirkwood portrayed this latter version of herself in the original production at select performances.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2023, it was announced that Kirkwood would write a new musical adaptation of [Roald Dahl's](/wiki/Roald_Dahl "Roald Dahl") *[The Witches](/wiki/The_Witches_(musical) "The Witches (musical)")* at London’s [National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") with music by [Dave Malloy](/wiki/Dave_Malloy "Dave Malloy") and lyrics by both Malloy and Kirkwood.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The musical premiered in November 2023.

### Screenwriting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucy_Kirkwood&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Screenwriting")]

Kirkwood wrote for the second, third, and fourth series of the teen drama *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(TV_series) "Skins (TV series)")*, broadcast between 2008 and 2010.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2014, her drama series *[The Smoke](/wiki/The_Smoke_(TV_series) "The Smoke (TV series)")*, made by [Kudos](/wiki/Kudos_(production_company) "Kudos (production company)"), was televised on Sky 1.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

She adapted her play *Chimerica* into a [four-part miniseries](/wiki/Chimerica_(miniseries) "Chimerica (miniseries)") for [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"), broadcast in 2019, and then wrote the four-part series *[Adult Material](/wiki/Adult_Material "Adult Material")*, following a woman's life in the [adult film industry](/wiki/Adult_film_industry "Adult film industry").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Recognition and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucy_Kirkwood&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recognition and awards")]

In June 2018 Kirkwood was elected Fellow of the [Royal Society of Literature](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Literature "Royal Society of Literature") in its "40 Under 40" initiative.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Theatre
